speaker: interviewer
text: It is July 20th, 1995. This is tape number three. We're in Sunrise, Florida. And we're interviewing Samuel Klasner. Tell me about your relationship with Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had-- the first time I have him, I was working in the factory. And I have-- a lathe machine is automatic. When you put it on, it works by itself and stopped. In time, I was making myself a sweater-- I knitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knitted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came around-- it was at night-- he came around and stays behind me and looks at what I'm doing, and doesn't say a word. I don't see, because there was not a mirror to see if somebody's behind me. So after a while, he said, what are you doing. I told him. I said, I don't have it, a sweater, it's cold, and I make myself this. He said, just watch it, the guys doesn't come in and catch you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very-- he looks it-- very polite. And that's it, and I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his personality like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see not a bad personality, but he likes it-- he likes his womens, he likes money. And he was a businessman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All his SS people, where he was in camp there, they were afraid for him. When he said something, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they afraid of Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid they're going to ship on the camp they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of the other people that you met while in the factory under Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: German people?

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was-- I can't say we don't have it special that we went after somebody or something like this, something like it. We used to keep on and have in mind that, someday, we're going to be free. And everybody would say the same thing. Well, we talked with the women, because it was not separate barracks. We couldn't see-- in the factory, we could see each other. And couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Oskar Schindler's wife? Can you tell me about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She really-- I, personally, mine opinion, she was a doll. When I saw her on the dinner that we have, I bought her flowers. I presented flowers to her. I said to her, you don't mind, I'm going to call you the second mother. She start to cry. And I kiss her. And she was not sitting with me, because she remember me when I wasn't in camp there. She said she remember me because you have blue eyes-- the blue eyes, I remember. And she was doing very, very much without him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do? What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were sick-- doctors, special doctors, we didn't have it there. So she was helping with medication. Then she has it-- they were brought it in from the street as a Gentile, and you were a Jew from France, from Belgium. They took him in, in this camp, and they want to shoot him. The German, they want to shoot him. She don't let.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that she had a great humanitarian sense about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of the other people you met there that you still remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember very much-- all people was together, but so many years, you can't remember, because you don't have-- you're not in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the musicians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, two brothers from Krakow, and they were in our camp. The younger brother was my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Willi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Willi what? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Willi Rosen. Him, I can't forget. So one has an accordion and one has a drum. They were playing, we were singing, we were laughing. And they were very nice. They took-- when we came to Gross Rosen. And then from Gross Rosen, they send us to Brunnlitz. And the accordion and the drum doesn't come. They send this to Auschwitz. So he went and he bought it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that? Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, she cannot go in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So Oskar Schindler brought back the accordion and the drum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then we have music. They were playing, at least--

speaker: interviewer
text: Something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can you tell me about your period at this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, special-- cannot too much talk, because we was a short time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year and a half, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you hear rumors that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't hear, because we was not in contact with the Czechs or somebody else. Germans wouldn't tell you. But was a little sound, and Mr. Schindler said, it's not going too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Be much longer. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] How did you feel about being under the protection of Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel more-- more-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] safety.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was different than being in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. In Plaszow, you-- so you were sleeping in the barrack or you went out from the barrack, you don't know you're going to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had a sense of safety and security?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people in-- on the Schindler list that ended up being lovers or getting married? Or were there any romantic relationships between anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just keep company between them, but not like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any time that you felt that your life was threatened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not while under the care of Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we really looked away, and we looked-- should come the time already to liberate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a sense of that from anything in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it come closer to liberation? How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we was-- all of us couldn't be in touch with Oskar Schindler. He had somebody what he must've told. So we find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was rumors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did this make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, when I'm here I don't believe it. If I'm going to be on the other side, then I'm going to believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wait for the time that should become and liberate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the factory at this time? It was nearing the end of the war. Did production slow down?

speaker: interviewee
text: It slowed down-- before, it slowed down. He don't want to produce so much. He kicked back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he purposely stopped the production.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. He send them different-- all together knowing they cannot use it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we saw something. We smelled something that's coming sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of sabotaging the artillery that was being produced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't realize that anything that was being manufactured was faulty?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Life has to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he protected you from that as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you toward the end of the war, in the factory? How did you end up being liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were staying outside the barracks. And across the street was an open view that we could see what's going on on the other side. We saw when the Russians start to come with the tanks. And there was a flag-- Russian flag with everything, so we know we're all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the feeling in the factory at the time, in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was laughing, singing. And we kissed each other, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Mr. and Mrs. Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she has went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to Prague. He don't stay to the land of the-- when the Germans came in. I mean, the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left you behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left us behind, but he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for him to come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see him-- after a couple days, we see him. But still, I left and I don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody realize when he left that you were going to be liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just waiting for the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we was talking about this. And we say he went away. And we want to sign it-- we signed papers for him. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousand-- thousand-- she don't took off the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousand signature.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of paperwork was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just give them papers that he was good, and, because of him, we survived. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he request this paperwork?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we started-- between us, we started, because we don't want them that they should kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did the Russians liberate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 10th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945? And you saw the troops and the tanks approaching the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come up to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they passed. They chased the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you after that event?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I-- I said, I'm going to home. So that's why I get this papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this paper. What does this paper say?

speaker: interviewee
text: This paper said that I was working by Schindler in the factory. This is all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this paper supposed to do? Why did you have to get this paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have no passport, we didn't have no papers. They don't know who I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you needed identification papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Identification, so they gave this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Schindler gave you identification papers, so that you could go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would've happened if you didn't have this paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't have it, you don't have it. We have to go. But still, they would maybe go to the office and write out some papers, sign, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you decided to go back home, what was your condition-- physical condition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was not bad. I was-- sick, I was not sick. I was healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what had happened at home, or if you would find anybody back in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found cousins [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when you got to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of Poles were there like me. They call me in, in the house. They give me food. And I was a few-- a couple days, two, three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What condition did you find your town to be in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found in the same condition because they don't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't destroy the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they don't destroy. Some, a couple houses, it's nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find your mother, and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have a witness. Everybody said, I think they killed her. But they don't know. There was a guy, a Polish, and he went to Bergen-Belsen. He went to Treblinka. And he said it-- that nobody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he witnessed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters, and brothers, and mother taken out on transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took my younger sister and the mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The others, they were all married. Two was in Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had the oldest two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that your mother and sister went to Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they sent from this camp, Plaszow, they send it to Treblinka. Over there, in crematorium, they finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found that your mother and sister went to Plaszow, and from Plaszow, were sent to Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they went-- they don't went to Plaszow. They were in Wolbrom--

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took them in the cattle's train, cattle train. They took them and they send them like this, straight out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to your hometown, did you find any Jews left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Not even a son of a Jew was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody had been deported, and the town had been liquidated, and was totally rid of Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not liquidated for the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Jews, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came to look here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these the only surviving relatives you were able to find?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Wolbrom? How long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days. I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I heard they killed-- they called him the name [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a Polish thing, where they killed the Jewish. And if they say they are there, you forget. You're not alive anymore. You can't survive from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were still having pogroms against the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Where did you go from Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Wolbrom, I go to Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not long. I think a couple-- a week or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you pick Sosnowiec to go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: But over there, the Jewish coming back, too. So I thought maybe, over there, that I'd find somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Sosnowiec were did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. I went over there. I was looking around because my brothers lived in stores and houses. So they took the store, and they don't let you in to look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you upon seeing you, and knowing that you were the brother of the former shopkeeper?

speaker: interviewee
text: They treat me not bad, but not good, too. They look very bad on me that I came back. They said [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means, you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're still alive?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So they weren't happy to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not going-- all right, they know this I was not a millionaire. I don't hide the money. Some of them, they came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They don't have money-- they have money hide. So they killed them and they took the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the condition of the stores when you found them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they have it-- they have everything to sell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Yeah, that is-- that is life, what they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You realized, at that point, that your brothers were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't expect it they can survive. I listened what's going on and everything, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went, I pick up myself, and I went to Germany-- west Germany. This is occupied under United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from Poland to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: By walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have proper clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have food, but, still, I went-- we passed the [NON-ENGLISH] to Germany, west Germany. And after there, we went close to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say we, were you with somebody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Polish guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two of you were walking over.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was in-- we went to Regensburg. I was in Regensburg to 1949, August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there for almost four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from over there, I went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Regensburg, how did you take care of yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was like I joined, like here. We used to get help. And, sometimes, you know, I went out, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sometimes, I went out, got American soldiers that bought it, cognac. They liked to drink. You know, I make black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
